voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its hearing is keen and may help it sense movement in the canopy.
Its eyesight is weak but can detect shapes and light changes.
Its nose detects food and other sloths through smell.
Its curved claws feel the tension and texture of each branch it grips.
Where does it live — and why?: It spends almost its entire life hanging upside down in the rainforest canopy.
Dense tree cover provides both food and camouflage from eagles and ocelots.
It descends to the ground only once a week to defecate.
Green algae that grows in its fur helps it blend into leafy branches.
How does it send messages?: It is mostly silent but hisses and moans when threatened.
It slashes with its long claws as a defense when grabbed.
Stillness itself is a signal — staying motionless is how it avoids detection.
A curled body position in the branches signals that it is resting safely.
What does it do when something changes?: When disturbed, it moves slowly to a denser cluster of branches.
It will bite and claw if it cannot escape a predator.
Louder sounds cause it to slow its movements further or stay completely still.
It changes trees when its food source in one tree is used up.
How does it learn?: A young sloth clings to its mother for up to a year after birth.
It learns which trees and leaves to eat by riding with its mother.
Young sloths gradually explore nearby branches while staying within the mother's range.
How does it have babies?: A female gives birth to a single young after about ten months of gestation.
The infant clings to the mother's belly and is carried everywhere she goes.
The mother gives birth while hanging upside down in the canopy.
Young sloths cling tightly and learn the canopy slowly over many months.
What are its limits?: Its very low metabolic rate means it produces little body heat.
On the ground it is nearly helpless against predators.
Loss of rainforest trees removes both its food and its hiding places.
Very loud disturbances can disrupt its activity and foraging patterns.
What does this teach us?: Being slow and still is a survival strategy, not a weakness.
Its fur, posture, and algae growth all work together to hide it from predators.
A sloth's survival depends on a whole forest — not just one branch.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Spends almost its entire life in the trees, only descending roughly once a week to defecate.
Has algae growing in its fur, providing visual camouflage in the leaves.
Very low body-heat production is a core part of its biology, not a bug.
Gives birth while hanging upside down in the branches.
still_debated: How much of the weekly descent is ritual vs. flexible under pressure.
The exact ecological role of the fur-algae-moth symbiosis.
Whether apparent stillness reflects low cognition or deliberate concealment.
common_failure_modes: Treating slowness as weakness rather than as a working predator-avoidance strategy.
Assuming all sloth species have the same diet or daily rhythm (two-toed vs. three-toed differ).
Describing sloths on the ground as 'normal' — ground exposure is a high-risk state.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not confuse two-toed (Choloepus) with three-toed (Bradypus) sloths.
Never describe it as upright or ground-living in day-to-day life.
Do not claim it 'rarely moves' — it moves constantly, just slowly.
